insight: Grimbotch is most effective as a peak attacker when bought with the intent of soaking, because that avoids its lifespan drawback. It is relatively weak before breaking the absorb barrier due to its short lifespan, and mediocre during economy buildup since it will need replacement later, though buying a few during buildup is still acceptable given its short-term efficiency.
author: .bky_1556
context: Discussion comparing Grimbotch and Electrovore as attackers and when each is most effective.

insight: When multiple Grimbotches are about to expire simultaneously, holding them all allows the opponent to deny absorb. To avoid this, mixing in other attackers like Electrovore or Tarsier after breaking the absorb barrier (e.g., Grimbotch+Electrovore or Grimbotch+Tarsier instead of 2x Grimbotch) is strategically preferable, as it makes the opponent's defense more awkward.
author: .bky_1556
context: Expert analysis of Grimbotch lifespan management and attack sequencing around the absorb barrier.

insight: Colorless attackers are considered a forbidden or highly problematic design because they can be deployed without any warning (no colored resource requirement telegraphs the threat). A long build time (as seen with Savior) is the recognized fix for this problem.
author: .bky_1556
context: Discussion of a custom unit (Pawn) that was a colorless attacker, prompting analysis of why colorless attackers are problematic in Prismata's design.

insight: Click order matters when assigning attack damage during a breach. For example, carefully sequencing clicks to bring a Gauss Cannon to exactly 3 HP before sniping it with Apollo requires precise assignment of attack, making click order strategically significant in breach situations.
author: supergreyman
context: Discussion of click order and whether performing actions in different orders changes game outcomes, with a specific Gauss Cannon/Apollo example.

insight: A version of Deadeye Operative costing 11BB with supply 4, 4 health, start-of-turn gain 2 attack, and a click to destroy a non-blocking drone was considered completely degenerate. In practice, P2 had easy wins by going dd/dd/dbb into these units, because the unit forced out a Wall and then sniped drones once the Wall was deployed — an effect described as a 'free Auride Core' that combined reasonable efficiency with drone removal.
author: masn6811
context: Expert discussion of a historical overpowered Deadeye Operative stat line used as a cautionary example during custom unit design.

insight: Units that immediately threaten more attack than the number of colored resources in their cost are described as 'shock' units and are considered problematic. Colorless attackers must therefore have a build time of at least 2 to prevent this kind of instant undefendable threat.
author: masn6811
context: Expert analysis of unit design principles around shock threat and colorless attackers during a custom unit design discussion.

insight: 'SDR violation' (Strategically Dominant Response) refers to a unit or mechanic that incentivizes the opponent not to do what they would normally want to do in response. Apollo is cited as mildly violating SDR, and a proposed unit (Coconut) that made blocking with large units like Odin undesirable was flagged as a clear SDR violation.
author: .bky_1556
context: Design review of a custom unit that would punish the opponent for blocking with high-value units, prompting discussion of the SDR concept.

insight: Galvani Drone-style units (which consume Energy to produce attack) should cost approximately 2 gold less than their non-Galvani counterparts, following the same logic as Galvani Drone itself costing roughly the same as a regular Drone when paired with an Engineer. For example, a Galvani Cannon analog should cost around 4GE rather than 2GE.
author: .bky_1556
context: Community design discussion evaluating the pricing of custom Galvani-variant attacker units compared to existing units like Shredder.

insight: Attack-on-buy is considered a forbidden mechanic in Prismata unit design because it makes the sword (attack) icon on the unit inaccurate — the unit would appear to have ongoing attack when in fact it already fired it upon purchase.
author: .bky_1556
context: Discussion of a custom spell (Gear Change) that granted attack on buy, prompting clarification of why attack-on-buy is a disallowed design pattern.

insight: Zemora Voidbringer sets are characterized by the Zemora player typically generating no impressive attack output until the Zemora arrives, but they have little difficulty surviving until that point. At worst they can live but cannot build many Conduits while defending, and this tradeoff is considered the key strategic tension of Zemora.
author: mqp
context: Expert commentary on the strategic role of Zemora Voidbringer used as a design comparison reference during a custom unit discussion.

insight: Antima Comet is considered one of the better large burst-threat units in the game because it creates interesting gameplay without being overly rules-heavy. Unlike Wincer or frostbite units, Antima telegraphs itself in a way that is nonetheless exciting rather than plodding, and designers are encouraged to significantly differentiate any new burst designs from it.
author: http404error
context: Design discussion of a custom burst-threat unit, prompting http404error to compare it to existing burst units and offer design guidance.
